'''Bradley Godden''' (born 13 July 1969) is an Australian former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who played in the 1990s. He played for the [[Newcastle Knights]], [[Hunter Mariners]], and the [[Leeds Rhinos]] as a {{rlp|fb|wg}} or {{rlp|ce}}.

==Playing career==

===Australia===
Godden started his first grade career with the Newcastle Knights, playing from the bench in a 30–0 loss to the [[Parramatta Eels]] at [[Parramatta Stadium]] in round 14 of the [[1991 NSWRL season]]. He played just four games for the Knights in 1991, scoring his first try from fullback in round 19 at [[Marathon Stadium]] in a 32–16 win over the [[South Sydney Rabbitohs]].

Godden's form at fullback for the Knights was one of the reason's Newcastle qualified for their first finals series in [[1992 NSWRL season#Finals|1992]].{{citation needed|date=May 2017}} Representative players [[Mark Sargent]], [[Paul Harragon]], [[Michael Hagan]] and [[Robbie McCormack]] led the Knights to a 4th-placed finish in the regular season. They then accounted for [[Western Suburbs Magpies|Western Suburbs]] 21–2 in the minor preliminary semi-final, with Godden scoring just his third try for the season, before going down to eventual grand finalist [[St. George Dragons|St. George]] 2–3 in a rare low scoring encounter in the minor semi-final.

After starring at fullback for the Knights in the 1992 finals series, Godden gained selection for [[Australian Kangaroos|Australia]] for the [[1989–1992 Rugby League World Cup#Final|1992 World Cup]] in England. He played in one of the three preliminary matches on the tour, but wasn't selected for the final at [[Wembley Stadium (1923)|Wembley Stadium]], coach [[Bob Fulton]] preferring [[Balmain Tigers|Balmain]] youngster [[Tim Brasher]] instead.<ref>[https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/rugby-league-meninga-breaks-britains-resistance-australians-recover-their-full-power-to-shackle-the-resilient-lions-and-ensure-another-series-victory-in-the-third-and-deciding-test-1531190.html Meninga breaks Britain's resistance] (independent.co.uk)</ref>

Godden continued with the Knights until the end of the [[1996 ARL season|1996 season]]. He was able to stay in Newcastle though as a member of the new [[Hunter Mariners]] team who played in the new [[1997 Super League (Australia) season|Super League]] competition. However, the emergence of [[Robbie Ross (rugby league)|Robbie Ross]] saw Godden restricted to 11 games for the Mariners only season of existence. Godden also played for the Mariners in the [[1997 World Club Championship#Final|1997 World Club Championship]] Final against the [[Brisbane Broncos]] in [[Auckland]]. It was the Hunter Mariners final game as a club.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.totalrl.com/friday-flashback-1997-world-club-challenge/|title=Friday Flashback 1997 World Club Challenge|website=www.totalrl.com|date=August 2014 }}</ref>

===England===
Godden then linked with the [[Leeds Rhinos]] in 1998, playing centre in their [[1998 Super League Grand Final]] loss to [[Wigan Warriors]].<ref>{{cite news 
  | title = Wonderful Wigan add Final chapter to illustrious history
  | work = [[The Birmingham Post]]
  | place = England
  | date = 1998-10-26
  | url = https://www.questia.com/read/1G1-60696444
  | accessdate = 8 May 2017}}</ref> After playing at centre for Leeds throughout the 1999 season, where he scored seven tries in 24 games, Godden retired from playing at the age of 31. Godden's final game for Leeds was their [[1999 Challenge Cup]] final victory over the [[London Broncos]] which Leeds won 52–16 with Godden scoring a try in the game.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.rugby-league.com/article/57222/coral-challenge-cup-final-history--leeds-rhinos/|title=Coral Challenge Cup Final History Leeds Rhinos|website=www.rugby-league.com}}</ref>
